past_medical_history: Generalized anxiety disorder, diagnosed 4 years ago
Migraine without aura, episodic, since age 18
History of psychogenic seizures diagnosed at age 23 at an outside facility
One prior hospitalization for syncope 6 months ago, cardiac workup unrevealing
family_history: Mother with generalized anxiety disorder and fibromyalgia
No family history of epilepsy, stroke, or cardiac disease

social_history.recent_stressors: Recently separated from long-term partner 3 months ago; experiencing financial difficulties

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Affect mildly anxious but cooperative. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Attention and concentration intact. Memory intact for immediate, short-term, and long-term recall.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II-XII intact. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light bilaterally. Visual fields full to confrontation. No nystagmus. Facial sensation and strength symmetric. Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Sternocleidomastoid and trapezius strength 5/5 bilaterally. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Right lower extremity demonstrates give-way weakness with 4-/5 strength on direct testing but observed to use the limb normally when not being formally tested. Left lower extremity 5/5 throughout. Upper extremities 5/5 bilaterally. Normal bulk and tone throughout. Hoover sign positive on the right: involuntary hip extension noted when testing contralateral hip flexion against resistance.
neurological_exam.sensory: Reports decreased sensation to light touch over the entire right leg in a non-dermatomal distribution with a sharp midline split. Vibration and proprioception intact bilaterally. Pin prick testing inconsistent with repeat examinations showing variable boundaries.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin intact on the left. On the right, performance is inconsistent — normal on distraction but effortful on direct testing.
neurological_exam.gait: Demonstrates dragging of the right leg with inconsistent pattern. When distracted by conversation, gait normalizes transiently. No Trendelenburg sign. Romberg test shows dramatic swaying without falling, with self-correction.
neurological_exam.additional: During examination, patient had a witnessed episode of bilateral upper extremity tremor with eye closure lasting approximately 90 seconds. Tremor was distractible with entrainment testing — frequency changed when asked to tap contralateral hand at a different rate. No post-event confusion. No tongue-biting. No incontinence.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of shaking and right leg weakness for the past 12 months
patient.history_present_illness: A 26-year-old right-handed woman presents to the neurology inpatient service with a 12-month history of recurrent episodes of shaking and subjective right leg weakness. The episodes began approximately one year ago and have been increasing in frequency, now occurring several times per week. Each episode consists of bilateral upper extremity tremor, sometimes progressing to whole-body shaking, lasting between 30 seconds and 3 minutes. She reports that the episodes are sometimes preceded by a sense of anxiety or lightheadedness, but often occur without clear warning. She occasionally loses awareness during the events, though bystanders report that she appears to respond to voice during some episodes. She has not sustained injuries during these events and denies tongue-biting or urinary incontinence. Between episodes, she has noticed progressive weakness of her right leg, which she describes as a heaviness and difficulty lifting the limb. The weakness fluctuates throughout the day and is sometimes worse with stress. She reports that the right leg occasionally gives way, causing her to stumble, though she has not fallen. She also describes intermittent decreased sensation over the right leg. Three months ago, she separated from her long-term partner and has been under significant financial stress. Her anxiety has worsened over this period. She was previously told at an outside hospital that she had psychogenic seizures, but she is uncertain about this diagnosis and seeks further evaluation. She denies fevers, weight loss, visual changes, dysphagia, or bowel or bladder dysfunction.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 10 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric, well-regulated, reactive to eye opening
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles captured during drowsiness

findings.clinical_correlation: During the recording, the patient had a clinical event consisting of bilateral upper extremity tremor with eye closure lasting approximately 75 seconds. The EEG showed no ictal correlate: the background rhythm remained intact throughout the event with normal alpha activity and no epileptiform discharges, electrodecrement, or post-event slowing. This is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES).

artifacts.description: Rhythmic movement artifact present during the clinical event, consistent with tremor, no underlying epileptiform activity obscured

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. No epileptiform abnormalities provoked. No habitual event triggered.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at standard frequencies (1-30 Hz). Normal photic driving response. No photoparoxysmal response.

eeg.impression: NORMAL EEG with capture of habitual clinical event. The clinical episode of bilateral upper extremity tremor occurred without any EEG correlate, consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic seizure (PNES). No epileptiform discharges were seen. Background activity is normal and symmetric.
eeg.limitations: Single routine recording; prolonged monitoring could capture additional events.
recommended_actions: Clinical correlation with neurological examination findings
Consider video-EEG monitoring for further event characterization if clinically indicated
Multidisciplinary evaluation including psychiatry and psychology

additional_observations: Normal basal ganglia and thalami bilaterally
Orbits, sinuses, and mastoids are unremarkable
mri.impression: NORMAL brain MRI. No structural abnormality identified. No evidence of stroke, demyelination, mass lesion, or other pathology to explain the patient's symptoms.
recommended_actions: No further neuroimaging indicated based on current findings

ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No acute or chronic abnormalities detected.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG. No cardiac arrhythmia or conduction abnormality to account for syncopal episodes.

Seizure_Workup.clinical_significance: Not elevated post-event. In epileptic seizures, prolactin typically rises 2-3x above baseline within 10-20 minutes. Normal post-event prolactin supports non-epileptic etiology.

labs.interpretation: All laboratory values within normal limits. CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, and hepatic panel are unremarkable. Post-event prolactin level is not elevated, which argues against an epileptic seizure and is consistent with a psychogenic non-epileptic event.

background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture captured over 48-hour monitoring

findings.clinical_correlation: Three habitual events were captured over 48 hours. All consisted of bilateral upper extremity tremor with eye closure, variable duration (45-120 seconds). No EEG correlate during any event — normal alpha rhythm persisted throughout. Video review shows preserved awareness during events, distractible tremor, and no post-ictal confusion. All events are consistent with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES).

artifacts.description: Intermittent movement artifact during clinical events, not obscuring underlying normal background

output.impression: NORMAL prolonged video-EEG monitoring over 48 hours. Three habitual clinical events captured, all without EEG correlate. Findings are diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES). No interictal epileptiform discharges. Normal background throughout.
recommended_actions: Communicate PNES diagnosis to patient with empathy and clarity
Referral to neuropsychiatry or psychology for CBT-based therapy
Avoid anti-seizure medications as these are not epileptic events

EMG.value: Normal insertional activity, no spontaneous activity, normal MUP morphology and recruitment
Normal insertional activity, no spontaneous activity, normal MUPs
Normal insertional activity, normal MUPs, full recruitment

output.interpretation: Normal EMG and nerve conduction studies of the right lower extremity. No evidence of peripheral neuropathy, radiculopathy, plexopathy, or myopathy. Electrodiagnostic findings do not support a neuromuscular cause for the patient's right leg weakness.

signal_characteristics.T2: Normal signal intensity throughout the cervical cord

additional_observations: No disc herniation or spinal stenosis
Normal vertebral body alignment and marrow signal
Conus medullaris terminates normally at L1
output.impression: NORMAL MRI of the cervical and thoracic spine. No spinal cord lesion, demyelination, compressive pathology, or structural abnormality to explain the patient's symptoms.

Psychiatric_Assessment.reference_range: 0-4 minimal, 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Moderate depression. Recent relationship breakdown and financial stressors contributing to depressed mood, poor sleep, and reduced interest in activities.
Severe anxiety. Reports persistent worry, restlessness, and physical symptoms of anxiety including palpitations and tension.
Below threshold for PTSD. Some trauma-related symptoms present but not meeting full criteria.

Psychiatric_Assessment.value: Comorbid moderate depression and severe generalized anxiety in context of significant psychosocial stressors. History of somatization. No active suicidal ideation.
Psychiatric_Assessment.clinical_significance: Psychiatric comorbidities likely contributing to functional neurological symptoms. Recommend CBT and optimization of anxiolytic/antidepressant therapy.

output.interpretation: Formal psychiatric evaluation reveals moderate depression (PHQ-9: 14) and severe generalized anxiety (GAD-7: 16) in the context of recent significant psychosocial stressors. The psychiatric profile is consistent with comorbidities commonly associated with functional neurological disorder. Cognitive behavioral therapy and optimization of pharmacotherapy are recommended.

Neuropsychological_Testing.clinical_significance: Valid test results; no evidence of symptom exaggeration on formal effort testing

output.interpretation: Neuropsychological testing reveals average intellectual functioning with intact attention, processing speed, executive function, and memory. Performance validity testing was passed, indicating genuine effort. No cognitive deficits identified. The cognitive profile does not suggest a neurodegenerative or structural neurological process.

output.query: functional neurological disorder diagnosis positive signs criteria

results.title: Functional neurological disorder: new subtypes and shared mechanisms
results.authors: Espay AJ, Aybek S, Carson A, et al.
results.summary: FND is diagnosed by positive clinical signs, not merely by exclusion. Key positive signs include Hoover sign, tremor entrainment, distractibility of deficits, and inconsistency between formal testing and observed function.

results.title: Psychogenic non-epileptic seizures: a review
results.authors: LaFrance WC Jr, Baker GA, Duncan R, et al.
results.summary: PNES are diagnosed by video-EEG capturing habitual events without ictal EEG correlate. Features supporting PNES include: prolonged duration, fluctuating course, eye closure, pelvic thrusting, side-to-side head movements, and preserved awareness.

results.title: A new approach to the diagnosis of functional neurological disorder
results.authors: Stone J, Carson A, Hallett M
results.summary: DSM-5 criteria require positive neurological findings inconsistent with recognized neurological disease. FND should be a rule-in diagnosis based on examination findings such as Hoover sign, dragging gait, entrainment, and give-way weakness.

output.summary: Functional neurological disorder (FND) is diagnosed based on positive clinical signs demonstrating internal inconsistency, such as Hoover sign for weakness, tremor entrainment for tremor, and video-EEG without ictal correlate for psychogenic non-epileptic seizures. DSM-5 emphasizes that FND is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive exam findings, not a diagnosis of exclusion.

interactions: Sumatriptan: potential serotonin syndrome risk (moderate interaction). Monitor for symptoms of serotonin excess including agitation, tremor, hyperthermia.
warnings: Dose adjustment may be considered for anxiety optimization — current dose is at the lower end of the therapeutic range for GAD (10-20 mg)
alternatives: Sertraline 50-200 mg daily (alternative SSRI with similar evidence base for anxiety)
Duloxetine 60-120 mg daily (SNRI option, may help with comorbid pain complaints)

Autoimmune_Panel.clinical_significance: Rules out neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder

output.interpretation: Autoimmune panel is entirely negative. No serological evidence of systemic autoimmune disease, neuromyelitis optica, or MOG-associated disorder. Vitamin B12 is normal. Results do not support an autoimmune or inflammatory etiology for the patient's symptoms.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Functional neurological disorder (conversion disorder) with psychogenic non-epileptic seizures and functional limb weakness

differential.key_distinguishing: EEG showed no ictal correlate during captured events, no post-ictal confusion, no elevated post-event prolactin, distractible tremor with entrainment — all supporting non-epileptic etiology
Normal brain and spine MRI with no demyelinating lesions, normal inflammatory markers, no dissemination in time or space
Young age, no vascular risk factors, normal MRI without DWI restriction, weakness does not follow vascular territory, Hoover sign positive indicating functional etiology
Normal EMG/NCS, non-dermatomal sensory distribution with midline split, inconsistent weakness pattern, normal reflexes
No fatigable weakness, no ocular or bulbar symptoms, give-way pattern rather than fatigable pattern, Hoover sign not seen in myasthenia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG with event capture to evaluate shaking episodes
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal EEG with clinical event captured showing no ictal correlate, consistent with PNES

optimal_actions.action: Obtain brain MRI to exclude structural pathology
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal brain MRI without evidence of stroke, demyelination, or mass

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive labs including CBC, BMP, thyroid function, inflammatory markers, and post-event prolactin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: All labs normal, including non-elevated post-event prolactin supporting non-epileptic etiology

optimal_actions.action: Obtain ECG to evaluate for cardiac causes of episodic symptoms
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm, no arrhythmia or conduction abnormality

optimal_actions.action: Recognize positive functional neurological signs on exam: Hoover sign, give-way weakness, distractible tremor with entrainment, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Positive clinical signs confirming FND as a rule-in diagnosis

optimal_actions.action: Search medical literature for FND diagnostic criteria and management
optimal_actions.expected_finding: FND diagnosed by positive signs, not exclusion; CBT is first-line treatment

optimal_actions.action: Refer for psychiatric evaluation to assess comorbid anxiety and depression
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Moderate depression, severe anxiety — psychiatric comorbidities common in FND

critical_actions: Capture habitual event on EEG to confirm absence of ictal correlate
Identify positive functional signs on neurological exam (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment)
Diagnose FND as a rule-in diagnosis based on positive signs, not merely by exclusion of other conditions
Refer for psychological/psychiatric treatment (CBT is evidence-based for FND)
Communicate the diagnosis clearly and empathetically to the patient
contraindicated_actions: Starting anti-seizure medications (events are non-epileptic; ASMs would be ineffective and expose patient to side effects)
Ordering unnecessary invasive procedures such as lumbar puncture when there is no clinical indication
Dismissing symptoms as malingering or fabricated — FND involves genuine involuntary symptoms
Prescribing benzodiazepines for seizure-like events (risk of dependence, no benefit for PNES)
Repeated neuroimaging without new clinical indication
key_reasoning_points: The patient has positive functional neurological signs: Hoover sign (involuntary hip extension during contralateral hip flexion testing), give-way weakness, distractible and entrainable tremor, non-dermatomal sensory loss with midline split, and inconsistent gait that normalizes with distraction
EEG captured a habitual event without any ictal correlate, which is diagnostic of psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES) rather than epileptic seizures
Post-event prolactin was not elevated, further supporting non-epileptic etiology (prolactin typically rises 2-3x after generalized tonic-clonic or complex partial seizures)
Normal brain MRI excludes structural causes including stroke, MS, and mass lesion
Normal ECG excludes cardiac arrhythmia as a cause of episodic symptoms
The clinical history of anxiety, psychosocial stressors (relationship breakdown, financial stress), and prior diagnosis of psychogenic seizures supports the FND diagnosis
FND is a rule-in diagnosis based on positive clinical signs per DSM-5 criteria, not a diagnosis of exclusion

The source case originally presented with syncope and conduction abnormalities due to surreptitious loperamide abuse, but the patient's documented psychogenic seizure history represents the functional neurological component

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic positive functional neurological signs (Hoover sign, give-way weakness, tremor entrainment, non-dermatomal sensory loss) and captured PNES on EEG. Minimal confounders.
